title: Fixing HTML Headings with Invalid Tags

statement.markdown:
Write a function that takes a string representing a block of HTML code as input and returns the valid HTML heading tags. A valid heading tag is one that starts with ''. The function should ignore any HTML tags that do not meet this criteria. For example, if the input is '

Hello World

This is a paragraph.

', the function should return '

Hello World

'.
statement.inputFormat: A string representing a block of HTML code.
statement.outputFormat: A string representing the valid HTML heading tags.
statement.notes: The input string may contain multiple HTML tags, but only heading tags should be returned.

constraints.description: The number of valid heading tags.

examples.explanation:
Only the heading tag '

Hello World

' is returned.
Only the last heading tag is returned.

hints.text: Split the input string into individual tags.

editorial.title: Editorial
editorial.intuition: To solve this problem, we can use a loop to iterate over the input string and check each tag to see if it starts with '<h' and ends with '>'. If it does, we add it to the output string.
editorial.bruteForce: We can use a brute force approach to check each character in the input string to see if it is part of a heading tag.
editorial.optimalSolution: We can use a regular expression to match heading tags and extract them from the input string.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The regular expression '<h\d+>[^<]*</h\d+>' matches any heading tag. We can use this regular expression to extract all heading tags from the input string.
editorial.dryRun:
Let's say the input string is '

Hello World

This is a paragraph.

'. We can use the regular expression '[^<]*' to match and extract the heading tags.
commonMistakes: Not checking if the tag starts with '<h' and ends with '>'.
Not using a regular expression to match heading tags.
